tossups.question: This poet ended one poem by describing figures “of equal size” who “shout, dream, weep, sing”. That poem by this poet opens by describing “Shadows that only I can see”, and contrasts the title characters’ cries of (+) “I tire!” and “I am dying!”. In another poem, this poet described a figure with “eyes of glass”, who “coils on a stick” and “can move without feet” before instructing, “Hit him with an ax and he dies”. This poet repeatedly exclaimed (*) “Mayombe—bombe—mayombé” in a “Chant for Killing a Snake”. For 10 points, name this Cuban poet of “Ballad of the Two Grandfathers” and “Sensemayá”.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Nicolas Guillen [or Nicolas Cristobal Guillen Batista]
tossups.question_sanitized: This poet ended one poem by describing figures "of equal size" who "shout, dream, weep, sing". That poem by this poet opens by describing "Shadows that only I can see", and contrasts the title characters' cries of (+) "I tire!" and "I am dying!". In another poem, this poet described a figure with "eyes of glass", who "coils on a stick" and "can move without feet" before instructing, "Hit him with an ax and he dies". This poet repeatedly exclaimed (*) "Mayombe-bombe-mayombe" in a "Chant for Killing a Snake". For 10 points, name this Cuban poet of "Ballad of the Two Grandfathers" and "Sensemaya".

tossups.question: This thinker wrote an allegorical criticism of neoliberalism featuring a knight-errant beetle named Don Durito. Following backlash, the US National Endowment for the Arts canceled funding for a children’s book about the Mayan creation myth by this thinker entitled The Story of the Colors. In 2006, this thinker went on a rare public tour of his country as part of The Other Campaign, during which he called himself (*) Delegate Zero. To honor a murdered teacher, this thinker announced that he was “ceasing to exist” in 2014, adopting the new pseudonym “Galeano.” Born Rafael Sebastián Guillén Vicente, this thinker was a philosophy professor at UAM before debuting as his most famous persona during a 1994 uprising against NAFTA. For 10 points, a black ski mask and a pipe are the trademarks of what pseudonymous figurehead of the Zapatistas?
tossups.answer: Subcomandante Marcos [accept Rafael Sebastián Guillén Vicente until read; accept Subcomandante Galeano until “Galeano” is read]

packet.name: 04 - Mirkin et al, Cheng et al

tossups.answer_sanitized: Subcomandante Marcos [accept Rafael Sebastian Guillen Vicente until read; accept Subcomandante Galeano until "Galeano" is read]
tossups.question_sanitized: This thinker wrote an allegorical criticism of neoliberalism featuring a knight-errant beetle named Don Durito. Following backlash, the US National Endowment for the Arts canceled funding for a children's book about the Mayan creation myth by this thinker entitled The Story of the Colors. In 2006, this thinker went on a rare public tour of his country as part of The Other Campaign, during which he called himself (*) Delegate Zero. To honor a murdered teacher, this thinker announced that he was "ceasing to exist" in 2014, adopting the new pseudonym "Galeano." Born Rafael Sebastian Guillen Vicente, this thinker was a philosophy professor at UAM before debuting as his most famous persona during a 1994 uprising against NAFTA. For 10 points, a black ski mask and a pipe are the trademarks of what pseudonymous figurehead of the Zapatistas?

tossups.question: In a poem by an author from this group, the speaker sees an old man being “slowly borne off in the sun’s last rays, like so many other invisible things in the world.” A poet from this group wrote of a man who “wants to storm the bank, steal clouds, stars, golden comets, and to buy what is most difficult: the sky” in his poem “The Covetous Angel,” from his collection Concerning the Angels. A member of this group wrote “the horseman came nearer drumming across the plain” in the opening poem of a collection, which warns: “Run, (*) moon, moon, moon!” The author of Destruction or Love invoked his memory of being in this group upon being told he had won the 1977 Nobel Prize. A poet wrote a collection of ballads inspired by Roma people a year after convening with this group on the 300th anniversary of Luis de Gongora’s death. For 10 points, Rafael Alberti, Vicente Aleixandre, and Federico Garcia Lorca belonged to what circle of Spanish poets named for a year?
tossups.answer: Generation of ‘27 [or Generation of 1927; or Generación del 27; or Generation of 1925; or Generación del 25; or the Generation of the Dictatorship; or Generation of Avant-Gardes; or Generation of Friendship; accept Generation Guillén-Lorca before “Lorca”]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Generation of '27 [or Generation of 1927; or Generacion del 27; or Generation of 1925; or Generacion del 25; or the Generation of the Dictatorship; or Generation of Avant-Gardes; or Generation of Friendship; accept Generation Guillen-Lorca before "Lorca"]
tossups.question_sanitized: In a poem by an author from this group, the speaker sees an old man being "slowly borne off in the sun's last rays, like so many other invisible things in the world." A poet from this group wrote of a man who "wants to storm the bank, steal clouds, stars, golden comets, and to buy what is most difficult: the sky" in his poem "The Covetous Angel," from his collection Concerning the Angels. A member of this group wrote "the horseman came nearer drumming across the plain" in the opening poem of a collection, which warns: "Run, (*) moon, moon, moon!" The author of Destruction or Love invoked his memory of being in this group upon being told he had won the 1977 Nobel Prize. A poet wrote a collection of ballads inspired by Roma people a year after convening with this group on the 300th anniversary of Luis de Gongora's death. For 10 points, Rafael Alberti, Vicente Aleixandre, and Federico Garcia Lorca belonged to what circle of Spanish poets named for a year?

tossups.question: In 2005, this man invited Inter Milan to play a game of soccer against members of his organization, refereed by Diego Maradona, but the game didn’t end up happening. A toucan narrates the story of the gods’ invention of colors in a folktale for children written by this man. He considers the Cold War to be the Third World War and the struggle between capitalism and socialism to be the Fourth World War. He was referred to as “Delegate Zero” during a campaign based on the ideals set forth in the Sixth Declaration of the (*) Lacandon Jungle. This man traveled around his country in an anti-capitalist program called the Other Campaign. In 2014, this pipe-smoking, mask-wearing figure announced that he no longer existed. For 10 points, name this former spokesman of the Zapatista National Liberation Army.
tossups.answer: Subcomandante (Insurgente) Marcos (or Subcomandante Insurgente Galeano; prompt on “Rafael Sebastián Guillén Vicente”)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Subcomandante (Insurgente) Marcos (or Subcomandante Insurgente Galeano; prompt on "Rafael Sebastian Guillen Vicente")
tossups.question_sanitized: In 2005, this man invited Inter Milan to play a game of soccer against members of his organization, refereed by Diego Maradona, but the game didn't end up happening. He considers the Cold War to be the Third World War and the struggle between capitalism and socialism to be the Fourth World War. He was referred to as "Delegate Zero" during a campaign based on the ideals set forth in the Sixth Declaration of the (*) Lacandon Jungle. This man traveled around his country in an anti-capitalist program called the Other Campaign. In 2014, this pipe-smoking, mask-wearing figure announced that he no longer existed. For 10 points, name this former spokesman of the Zapatista National Liberation Army.

bonuses.leadin: A boy of this ethnicity is told, “Charity’s payin’ yo’ keep, / She’s givin’ you all you need” in the first poem of a collection titled for motifs in this ethnic group’s music. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A boy of this ethnicity is told, "Charity's payin' yo' keep, / She's givin' you all you need" in the first poem of a collection titled for motifs in this ethnic group's music. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this ethnic group. A poet from this ethnic minority imitated its music with onomatopoeia exemplified by the collection title Sóngoro cosongo and the line “Mayombe-bombe-mayombé” from a “chant to kill a snake.”
This Afro-Cuban poet and scholar of Nicolás Guillén (“ghee-YEN”) began successive stanzas “I rebelled,” “I walked,” and “I rose up” in her poem “Mujer negra,” or “Black Woman.”

In her poem “Cimarrones,” Morejón describes how it “dignifies our struggle / to go out and see us … sweeping shit / birthing [these things].” “Guantanamera” was adapted from a José Martí collection titled [these things] Sencillos (“sen-SEE-yohs”).
parts_sanitized: Name this ethnic group. A poet from this ethnic minority imitated its music with onomatopoeia exemplified by the collection title Songoro cosongo and the line "Mayombe-bombe-mayombe" from a "chant to kill a snake."
This Afro-Cuban poet and scholar of Nicolas Guillen ("ghee-YEN") began successive stanzas "I rebelled," "I walked," and "I rose up" in her poem "Mujer negra," or "Black Woman."

In her poem "Cimarrones," Morejon describes how it "dignifies our struggle / to go out and see us ... sweeping shit / birthing [these things]." "Guantanamera" was adapted from a Jose Marti collection titled [these things] Sencillos ("sen-SEE-yohs").
answers: Afro-Cubans [or Black Cubans or afrocubanos; prompt on Black or Cuban or negro; reject “Yoruba”] (The poems are “Negro bembón,” from the collection Motivos de son, and “Sensemayá.” Both are by Nicolás Guillén.)
answers_sanitized: Afro-Cubans [or Black Cubans or afrocubanos; prompt on Black or Cuban or negro; reject "Yoruba"] (The poems are "Negro bembon," from the collection Motivos de son, and "Sensemaya." Both are by Nicolas Guillen.)
Nancy Morejon
verses [accept versos; accept Simple Verses or Versos sencillos]

bonuses.leadin: Note to players: Two answers required. A form popular in these two countries is based on a rhythm called the baqueteo. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Note to players: Two answers required. A form popular in these two countries is based on a rhythm called the baqueteo. For 10 points each:
parts: Name these two countries. A poem from one of these two countries about killing a snake inspired a composer from the other to write a piece beginning with a bass clarinet trill in 4/8 time.

The baqueteo forms the basis for this musical form popular in Cuba and Mexico, which developed from the contradanza. Gustavo Dudamel popularized a piece called "[this musical form] No. 2" by Mexican composer Arturo Marquez.
This American composer wrote a piece for two pianos called Danzón Cubano and imitated the jarabe dance in El Salón México. He also wrote Appalachian Spring.
parts_sanitized: Name these two countries. A poem from one of these two countries about killing a snake inspired a composer from the other to write a piece beginning with a bass clarinet trill in 4/8 time.

The baqueteo forms the basis for this musical form popular in Cuba and Mexico, which developed from the contradanza. Gustavo Dudamel popularized a piece called "[this musical form] No. 2" by Mexican composer Arturo Marquez.
This American composer wrote a piece for two pianos called Danzon Cubano and imitated the jarabe dance in El Salon Mexico. He also wrote Appalachian Spring.
answers: Mexico AND Cuba [or United Mexican States AND Republic of Cuba; or Estados Unidos Mexicanos AND República de Cuba] [The poem is Nicolás Guillén's Sensemayá, which inspired a piece of the same title by Silvestre Revueltas.]
answers_sanitized: Mexico AND Cuba [or United Mexican States AND Republic of Cuba; or Estados Unidos Mexicanos AND Republica de Cuba] [The poem is Nicolas Guillen's Sensemaya, which inspired a piece of the same title by Silvestre Revueltas.]
danzon [or Danzon No. 2; prompt on dance]
Aaron Copland

bonuses.leadin: Two collections of Selected Poems, respectively translated by Doris Dana and Langston Hughes, appeared after this poet’s 1957 death. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this romantic partner of Dana. Hughes’s book opens with “Cradle Songs” drawn from this poet’s Desolación.

“Li’l Manuel” is critiqued for “all data English you used to know” in Hughes’s Black vernacular translation of a poem from this author’s Motives de son. Eartha Kitt recorded a reading of Hughes’s translation of this other Black poet’s “Chant for Killing a Snake.”

Hughes translated Masters of the Dew after the death of author Jacques Roumain, a Marxist from this home country of Edwidge Danticat (“dah-tee-KAH”). A revolution in this non-Cuban country is the setting for Alejo Carpentier’s The Kingdom of This World.
answers: Nicolas Guillen [or Nicolas Cristobal Guillen Batista] (The latter poem is Sensemaya.)
answers_sanitized: Gabriela Mistral [or Lucila Godoy Alcayaga]
Nicolas Guillen [or Nicolas Cristobal Guillen Batista] (The latter poem is Sensemaya.)
(Republic of) Haiti [or Republique d'Haiti; or Repiblik d Ayiti]

bonuses.leadin: This author drew on the rhythms of the musical genre son(“sone”) in his collections Motivos de son and El son entero(“en-TEH-roh”). For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poet and journalist of African descent. This author of Tengo and the onomatopoetic Sóngoro cosongo critiqued ongoing racial exploitation in West Indies Ltd(“limited”).
Guillén is a poet from this Caribbean country, where “Guantanamera” (“gwahn-tah-nah-MAIR-ah”) was written by national hero José Martí.
In an eight-line poem, Martí wrote that he grows this plant “in July as well as in January,” and would offer it as readily to a foe as to a friend. Your
answers: a white rose [or una rosa blanca; accept translated titles such as “I Grow A White Rose”; accept “Cultivo una rosa blanca”; prompt on rose or flower]
answers_sanitized: Nicolas Guillen ("gee-YEN") [or Nicolas Cristobal Guillen Batista]
Cuba

bonuses.leadin: In this novel, Macandal evades being tortured and burned alive by transforming into an insect and flying away. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel by Alejo Carpentier in which the slave Ti Noel presents a fantastic account of early Haitian history.

This poet described wanting to bring a flower to his lover at the end of his poem “Sometimes” or “A Veces.” He was the national poet of Cuba for over two decades under the Castro government, and his poem about a snake-killing ritual inspired the Silvestre Revueltas composition Sensemaya .
answers: The Kingdom of This World [or El reino de este mundo ]
answers_sanitized: The Kingdom of This World [or El reino de este mundo ]
Nicolas Guillen

parts_sanitized: Name this novel by Alejo Carpentier in which the slave Ti Noel presents a fantastic account of early Haitian history.

This poet described wanting to bring a flower to his lover at the end of his poem "Sometimes" or "A Veces." He was the national poet of Cuba for over two decades under the Castro government, and his poem about a snake-killing ritual inspired the Silvestre Revueltas composition Sensemaya .

bonuses.leadin: A major politician from this family, named after the Aztec emperor Cuauhtemoc (k'wow-TAY-mock), became the first elected mayor of Mexico City. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this family of Mexican politicians. Its most famous member was a Governor of Michoacan who carried out agrarian reforms and nationalized the oil industry.

Lazaro Cardenas’s son Cuauhtemoc served as a witness in negotiating the 1995 San Andres Accords with this masked university professor, who emerged from the jungle on horseback with conch-blowing bodyguards.

Cuauhtemoc Cardenas split with the PRI to run for president in 1988, but this type of event marred the election proceedings. Cardenas supporters claimed that “the system failed” in reference to this event, which allowed the PRI candidate to defeat Cardenas.
answers: an IBM computer crash during the voting tabulation [or equivalents]
answers_sanitized: Cardenas [or Lazaro Cardenas]
Subcomandante Marcos [or Rafael Guillen Vicente; or Delegate Zero]
an IBM computer crash during the voting tabulation [or equivalents]

bonuses.leadin: This novel opens with the emcee of the Tropicana welcoming the crowd in English and Spanish. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this mostly plotless novel, which features the wordplay of Bustrofedon, a dead character whose voice is presented on tape, and the adventures of the photographer Codac.

In Three Trapped Tigers, Guillermo Cabrera Infante parodied the writing styles of other Cuban authors in a section about the murder of this historical figure. In Animal Farm, this figure is represented by Snowball.

One of those parodies makes fun of this author, who was declared Cuba’s national poet by the Castro regime in 1961. He first gained fame with eight short poems based on Afro-Cuban “Son” music, and wrote the poem “Sensemaya.”
answers_sanitized: Three Trapped Tigers [or Tres Tristes Tigres]
Leon Trotsky
Nicolas Guillen
